evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go defines package-level mutable lifecycle state (`eventBus`, `healthLoopRunning`, `healthLoopStopFn`) that is directly toggled by `StartDockerHealthLoops`, `startDockerHealthLoop`, and `StopDockerHealthLoops`.

goseg/broadcast/state.go exposes package-level mutable defaults (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory`) and a global setter-style seam (`SetBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory`), which changes behavior for the whole package.

goseg/config/config_update_schema.go uses a hand-built patch DSL (`confPatchField`, `buildConfPatchField`, `newConfPatchField`, custom parsers) plus repetitive specialized parse functions for each field shape, creating a high structural overhead for config schema changes.

`config_update_schema.go` implements `parseStringSliceValue` by accepting `[]interface{}` and converting every element with `fmt.Sprint`, so non-string payload values are silently coerced instead of rejected as invalid.
`config_update_registry.go` routes `piers` through that parser, so a malformed config patch can report success while storing implicitly stringified values in `Connectivity.Piers`.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Enforcing strict element typing for string-slice config patches (and adding targeted regression tests) would improve predictability of patch validation across config update boundaries.

findings.summary: Subsystem lifecycle control uses shared package globals, preventing independent lifecycle composition and creating implicit coupling between callers.
evidence: `subsystem_events.go` keeps the active event loop state in package globals (`eventBus`, `healthLoopRunning`, `healthLoopStopFn`) rather than in a runtime object.
`StopDockerHealthLoops` mutates and resets global flags directly, and `StartDockerHealthLoops/startDockerHealthLoop` also mutate the same shared state.

`broadcast/state.go` applies the same pattern at the broadcast subsystem level with `defaultBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory` and `SetBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory`, making subsystem behavior globally mutable.
There is no local abstraction boundary around lifecycle/session state, so sequential or concurrent start/stop flows can interfere across test and runtime invocations.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit runtime-scoped structs for subsystem orchestration that hold event bus and loop state per instance, and pass them explicitly through start/stop APIs; remove package-level mutable lifecycle and factory storage or keep it only in test-only build seams.

findings.summary: Broadcast state runtime resolution hides a global seam and can silently couple callers via mutable defaults.
evidence: `broadcast/state.go` stores a mutable `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` singleton and mutable `defaultBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory`.
`resolveDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`/`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` route through the mutable factory path.
`SetBroadcastStateRuntimeFactory` allows package-wide replacement of core behavior, which is a global mutable seam rather than dependency injection.
This design can mask ownership and initialization intent, especially as multiple callers/tests may depend on the same package-wide default.
findings.suggestion: Replace the global runtime factory seam with explicit constructor injection (e.g., pass a runtime provider into orchestration entry points) and keep default construction localized to composition/root initialization.

findings.summary: Config patch parsing/building grows through repetitive parser-registration patterns instead of a single declarative schema model.
evidence: The file defines `ConfPatch` plus many domain-specific parser functions (`parseBoolValue`, `parseIntValue`, `parseStringSliceValue`, `parseSessionMap`, `parseDiskWarningMap`, etc.).
Each new config patch field needs synchronized updates across type structs, parser field registration, and validation wiring in the same module.
Tests in `config_test.go` rely on broad behavioral contracts for field parsing rather than a compact schema-driven source of truth.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a declarative patch-spec model (field metadata + parser + merge/apply strategy in one descriptor) and generate/drive registration, parsing, and tests from that descriptor to avoid drift across hand-maintained registries.

findings.summary: Configuration patch parser silently stringifies non-string piers instead of returning a typed validation error.
evidence: `parseStringSliceValue` returns `append(parsed, fmt.Sprint(item))` for each `[]interface{}` entry, so values like `1` or `true` become `"1"`/`"true"` rather than failing.
`piers` in `allConfPatchFields()` is parsed using this helper, then applied into `ConnectivityConfig.Piers []string`.
findings.suggestion: Tighten `parseStringSliceValue` to validate each element is a string (or explicitly document and narrow supported types), return a typed parse error on invalid element types, and add a patch test for `{"piers":[123]}`/`{"piers":[true]}` in `config_update_schema` tests.
